title: Radiation-Area Surveys, Restricted Areas, and Posting
narrationScript: Every radiographic operation starts with the same two questions: what type of radiation is present, and how much of it. Your source and its scatter set the type; a calibrated survey meter sets the quantity, as dose rate. From those numbers you build the restricted area — the line you control so that no member of the public can receive more than two millirem in any one hour beyond it. Inside that line sit zones with legal definitions. Past five millirem in an hour at thirty centimeters, you are in a radiation area. Past one hundred, a high radiation area. Each carries exact posting: the magenta-on-yellow trefoil with the precise words the regulations demand. Calculation predicts where those lines fall; only walking the full perimeter with a meter proves it. And through every exposure, a qualified crew member keeps continuous direct visual surveillance of the boundary. If anyone crosses, the source goes back into its shield first — everything else comes second.

sections.heading: Survey First: What Radiation, and How Much?
sections.body:
Before the first exposure on any site, 10 CFR 20.1501 hands you a plain-language duty: make surveys that are reasonable under the circumstances to evaluate the **magnitude and extent of radiation levels** and the potential hazards present. For a radiographer that breaks into two questions.

**What type?** Your source defines it. Iridium-192 and cobalt-60 emit penetrating gamma; an X-ray crew deals with machine radiation that stops when the tube is de-energized; neutron work adds a hazard ordinary gamma instruments cannot see. Around any gamma shot there is also **scattered radiation** — photons that have bounced off the part, the ground, and the air, arriving from directions the beam never pointed and at lower energies than the source emits. Your instrument must suit the type and energy present, which is exactly why the detector and energy-response lessons came first.

**How much?** Quantity means **dose rate**, read in mR/h on a calibrated, operable meter — and for radiography that meter must read from **2 mR/h up through 1 R/h** (10 CFR 34.25), a range chosen deliberately: the top end covers readings near the device, and 2 mR/h is the level you will chase at the boundary. The numbers you measure — never the numbers you assume — are what every boundary, posting, and surveillance decision in this lesson stands on.

sections.heading: Restricted Areas: The Line You Control
sections.body:
Part 20 defines a **restricted area** as an area to which access is **limited by the licensee** for the purpose of protecting individuals against undue risks from radiation and radioactive materials. An **unrestricted area** is everything else — anywhere access is neither limited nor controlled. The boundary between them is yours to establish, and two public dose limits (10 CFR 20.1301) dictate where it must fall:

- **100 mrem (1 mSv) in a year** total effective dose equivalent to any individual member of the public, and
- **2 mrem (0.02 mSv) in any one hour** in any unrestricted area from external sources.

The working rule of field radiography: set the rope so a person standing at it through a full hour of your operation cannot receive more than 2 mrem. Note carefully what that says — dose **in any one hour**, not instantaneous dose rate. If the source is exposed only 15 minutes out of each hour, a spot reading 8 mR/h during shots accumulates just 2 mrem in the hour. Many companies simply rope off where the meter reads **2 mR/h during an exposure** — conservative, simple to verify, impossible to argue with. Your operating procedures state which approach your license uses.

Inside the rope: trained, monitored personnel under your control, and no one else. Outside it: the public, who get full protection without ever knowing you were there.

sections.caption: Nested zones around a field exposure: the high radiation area (could exceed 100 mrem in 1 hour at 30 cm), the radiation area (could exceed 5 mrem in 1 hour at 30 cm), and the restricted-area boundary set so the public cannot receive 2 mrem in any one hour. Distances are inverse-square planning estimates for an unshielded 100 Ci Ir-192 source.

sections.heading: Walking the Boundary
sections.body:
Plan the line with math; prove it with the meter.

**Calculate first.** A 100 Ci iridium-192 source, unshielded, produces about 0.5 R/h per curie at one meter — 50,000 mR/h at 1 m. The inverse square law puts the 2 mR/h line at √(50,000 ÷ 2) ≈ **158 m**. Uncollimated, your restricted area is a circle over 300 meters across. Add a collimator that cuts side and rear intensity by a factor of 100, and those directions pull in by √100 — to roughly **16 m**. That is why a collimator is boundary equipment as much as dose equipment.

**Then verify during the first exposure.** Inverse square assumes a point source in empty space; a real site has scatter off the part and the ground, **skyshine** scattered back down from the air above the shot, shadows cast by equipment, and beams pointed toward occupied buildings. Walk the **entire perimeter** with the meter while the source is out — every access point, every gap between obstacles — and think in three dimensions: floors above and below an elevated shot are part of the boundary too.

**Re-survey whenever the shot changes** — new source position, new beam direction, collimator on or off, boundary moved, stronger source. Yesterday's survey proves nothing about today's setup.

sections.caption: The nested areas around a radiographic operation — definitions from 10 CFR Part 20, tightest zone last.
rows: Unrestricted area
Public dose must stay under 2 mrem in any one hour and 100 mrem in a year
None
None — anyone may be present
Restricted area
Access limited by the licensee to protect people from radiation
No specific wording mandated for the boundary itself — barriers plus area signs in practice
Trained, monitored personnel admitted by the licensee
Radiation area
Could exceed 5 mrem (0.005 rem) in 1 hour at 30 cm
"CAUTION, RADIATION AREA"
Lies within the restricted boundary; posted at access points
High radiation area
Could exceed 100 mrem (0.1 rem) in 1 hour at 30 cm
"CAUTION, HIGH RADIATION AREA" or "DANGER, HIGH RADIATION AREA"
Control device, entry alarm, or locked entry — or continuous direct/electronic surveillance
Very high radiation area
Could exceed 500 rad (5 Gy) absorbed dose in 1 hour at 1 meter
"GRAVE DANGER, VERY HIGH RADIATION AREA"
Additional measures so no one can gain unauthorized or inadvertent access

sections.caption: Posting per 10 CFR 20.1902: prescribed wording with the radiation trefoil in magenta, purple, or black on a yellow background. Watch the very-high-radiation-area trap — its threshold changes quantity, unit, and distance: 500 rad absorbed dose in 1 hour at 1 meter.

sections.heading: Posting: Exact Words, Exact Colors
sections.body:
Posting requirements leave no room for creativity. The radiation symbol is the three-bladed **trefoil**, rendered in **magenta, purple, or black on a yellow background** (10 CFR 20.1901), and 10 CFR 20.1902 prescribes the words beside it:

- **Radiation area** — "CAUTION, RADIATION AREA"
- **High radiation area** — "CAUTION, HIGH RADIATION AREA" or "DANGER, HIGH RADIATION AREA"
- **Very high radiation area** — "GRAVE DANGER, VERY HIGH RADIATION AREA"

Signs must be **conspicuous**: posted at access points and spaced along the boundary so a person approaching from any direction meets a sign before meeting the radiation. A sign lying in the truck, facing the wrong way, or hidden behind a trailer satisfies nothing.

Part 20 grants two exceptions worth knowing precisely (10 CFR 20.1903). An area containing a source of radiation need not be posted for periods of **less than 8 hours** if the source is **constantly attended** by someone who can keep exposures within limits and the area is under the licensee's control — the provision that fits a transient field shot. And a room or area holding a **sealed source** needs no posting if the radiation level at **30 cm from the surface of the source container or housing** does not exceed **5 mrem (0.005 rem) per hour**. Exceptions excuse the sign — never the survey, the barrier, or the watching. Most operating procedures require posting every job, every time, and that is the habit to build.

sections.heading: High Radiation Areas: Watched or Locked — Never Locked In
sections.body:
Any location where a person could receive more than 100 mrem in an hour at 30 cm demands more than a sign. 10 CFR 20.1601 requires each entrance or access point to have at least one of:

- a **control device** that, upon entry, reduces the radiation level below the high-radiation-area threshold — the interlock of a permanent exposure room;
- a control device that energizes a **conspicuous visible or audible alarm** so both the person entering and the supervisor of the activity know immediately; or
- **locked entryways**, opened only when access is required, with positive control over each individual entry.

In place of those, the rule permits **continuous direct or electronic surveillance capable of preventing unauthorized entry** — and that substitution is how field radiography runs. 10 CFR 34.51 makes it explicit: during each radiographic operation, the radiographer or the other qualified individual required at temporary job sites (10 CFR 34.41) maintains **continuous direct visual surveillance** of the operation to protect against unauthorized entry into the high radiation area, unless that area is equipped with a control device or alarm system, or is locked.

Two absolutes ride along. Whatever controls you use may **never prevent a person from leaving** the area — egress stays free. And a **very high radiation area** (500 rad in 1 hour at 1 m) requires additional measures under 10 CFR 20.1602 so no one can gain unauthorized or inadvertent access at all. A typical field source does not meet that formal definition at one meter — but within arm's reach of an unshielded radiography source, dose rates run to hundreds of R/h. Treat the zone at the device with exactly that respect.

sections.title: Someone crosses the rope: source in first, words second
sections.body: Surveillance only protects if it triggers action. The instant anyone breaches the boundary during an exposure, **terminate the exposure** — crank the source back to its shielded position or cut power to the X-ray machine — confirm shutdown with your survey meter, and only then deal with the intruder. Never leave the controls to chase someone while the source is out, and never treat surveillance as something to glance at between other tasks: it is continuous, it is the assigned job of a qualified crew member, and at a temporary job site it is the only barrier with a brain. If the same gap lets people approach twice, the boundary is wrong — fix the boundary.

figures.title: Nested control zones around a field exposure
Required posting signs and their thresholds
Temporary job site plan view — surveillance exercise

interactive.title: Find the Boundary Failure
interactive.intro: This temporary job site is mid-exposure: a collimated Ir-192 shot on a pipe weld, rope boundary verified at 2 mR/h, operator at the controls. Four of these callouts show things done right — one violates the surveillance requirement. Click the failure.

regions.label: Boundary stretch behind the site trailer
regions.feedback: Correct. 10 CFR 34.51 requires continuous direct visual surveillance of the operation to protect against unauthorized entry into the high radiation area — and a boundary stretch you cannot see is a boundary you are not watching. The open, unposted path behind the trailer lets someone walk into the field mid-exposure with no sign to stop them and no eyes on them. Fix it before the next shot: close and post the gap, then either reposition the control point or station the second required crew member (10 CFR 34.41) where that stretch stays in view.

regions.label: Operator at the control point
regions.feedback: Nothing wrong here. The operator is at the crank with a calibrated meter in hand and most of the perimeter in view — exactly where a radiographer belongs during an exposure. The problem is the one stretch this position cannot see.

regions.label: Exposure device and drive cable
regions.feedback: The device setup is sound: a collimated shot with the drive cable run out to a control point well away from the source. The collimator shrinks the high-dose-rate zone dramatically — it is part of why this boundary can sit as close as it does.

regions.label: Posted sign on the north rope
regions.feedback: This sign is doing its job — radiation trefoil, the exact wording 10 CFR 20.1902 requires, facing approaching traffic at an access route. But remember that signs only warn; they cannot stop anyone. Keep looking for the place where the watching fails.

regions.label: Rope barrier on the west line
regions.feedback: The west rope is intact, posted, and inside the operator's line of sight. A continuous physical barrier with signs at intervals is exactly how a temporary restricted area should look. The failure is on the far side of the site.

checkQuestions.question: A survey during an exposure shows that a person at a certain spot could receive 150 mrem in 1 hour at 30 cm from the surface the radiation penetrates. By definition, that spot is in a:
options: Restricted area only — no further classification applies
Radiation area
High radiation area
Very high radiation area
checkQuestions.explanation: The high radiation area threshold is a dose equivalent that could exceed 100 mrem (0.1 rem) in 1 hour at 30 cm, and 150 mrem clears it. A very high radiation area requires the potential for 500 rad absorbed dose in 1 hour measured at 1 meter — a different number, unit, and distance.

checkQuestions.question: Which sign satisfies 10 CFR 20.1902 for a radiation area?
options: The radiation trefoil with the words "CAUTION, RADIATION AREA"
The radiation trefoil with the words "GRAVE DANGER, RADIATION AREA"
Any sign reading "RESTRICTED — KEEP OUT" in red letters
A yellow flag tied to the rope at each corner of the boundary
checkQuestions.explanation: Posting wording is prescribed, not invented: a radiation area is posted "CAUTION, RADIATION AREA" with the trefoil in magenta, purple, or black on yellow. "GRAVE DANGER" belongs only to very high radiation areas, and generic keep-out signs or flags satisfy nothing in Part 20.

checkQuestions.question: Field crews typically establish the restricted-area boundary so that:
options: the dose rate at the exposure device itself never exceeds 100 mR/h
a person remaining at the boundary could not receive more than 2 mrem in any one hour
the boundary encloses exactly the high radiation area and nothing more
the rope sits at least 500 feet from the source in every direction
checkQuestions.explanation: The governing public limits are 100 mrem in a year and 2 mrem in any one hour in unrestricted areas (10 CFR 20.1301), so the rope goes where a full hour of the operation cannot deliver 2 mrem — many companies conservatively place it where the meter reads 2 mR/h during an exposure. A fixed distance like 500 feet ignores source strength, collimation, and scatter.

checkQuestions.question: A crew is shooting at a temporary job site. The high radiation area is not locked and has no control device or entry alarm. What does 10 CFR 34.51 require?
options: Post additional DANGER signs every 10 feet along the rope
Survey the boundary once per hour and log the readings
Nothing further, provided the area was posted before the first exposure
Continuous direct visual surveillance of the operation by the radiographer or the other required crew member to prevent unauthorized entry
checkQuestions.explanation: At temporary job sites, surveillance substitutes for engineered controls: the radiographer or the second qualified individual must watch the operation continuously to protect against unauthorized entry into the high radiation area. Signs warn and surveys measure, but only the watching crew can actually stop an entry.

checkQuestions.question: An unshielded 100 Ci Ir-192 source produces about 50,000 mR/h at 1 m. Using the inverse square law, the radiation-area line — 5 mR/h — falls at about:
checkQuestions.explanation: d = √(50,000 ÷ 5) = √10,000 = 100 m. The 100 mR/h high-radiation-area line falls near 22 m and the 2 mR/h boundary near 158 m — and every one of those calculated lines must still be verified with a survey meter on the actual site, because scatter and obstructions move them.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Appendix A Radiographic Safety Course §3.1–3.3 (type and quantity of radiation, restricted areas, posting and surveillance)
10 CFR Part 20 §§20.1003, 20.1301, 20.1501, 20.1601–20.1602, 20.1901–20.1903 (definitions, public dose limits, surveys, access control, posting)
10 CFR Part 34 §§34.25, 34.41, 34.49, 34.51 (survey instruments, two-person crews, surveys, surveillance of radiographic operations)
NRC NUREG-1556, Vol. 2, Rev. 1 — Consolidated Guidance About Materials Licenses: Program-Specific Guidance About Industrial Radiography Licenses
